name: binary-re-tool-setup
description: Use when reverse engineering tools are missing, not working, or need configuration. Installation guides for radare2 (r2), Ghidra, GDB, QEMU, Frida, binutils, and cross-compilation toolchains. Keywords - "install radare2", "setup ghidra", "r2 not found", "qemu missing", "tool not installed", "configure gdb", "cross-compiler
capabilities: Install radare2, rabin2, and r2ghidra decompilation plugin
Set up Ghidra headless analyzer for batch processing
Configure QEMU user-space emulation for cross-architecture binary execution
Install GDB with multiarch support and GEF enhancements
Set up Frida for dynamic instrumentation on Linux ARM targets
Create cross-compilation sysroots for ARM and ARM64 analysis
author: 2389-research
securitySummary: Legitimate reverse engineering tool setup skill. Static scanner flagged many patterns (external commands, sudo, Docker --privileged) that are standard for cross-platform tool installation. All high-severity findings evaluated as FALSE_POSITIVES - patterns are expected for reverse engineering tool installation workflows.
valueStatement: Binary analysis requires properly installed tools. This skill provides installation guides for radare2, Ghidra, GDB, QEMU, and cross-compilation toolchains across Ubuntu, WSL2, macOS, and Arch Linux.
limitations: Does not execute tool commands - only provides installation documentation
Requires sudo/root access for system package installation
macOS QEMU support limited without Docker for full cross-arch emulation
GDB on macOS requires code signing for debugging support

useCases.targetUser: Security researchers starting a new project
useCases.title: New analysis environment setup
useCases.description: Get a fresh reverse engineering environment ready with all required tools for static and dynamic analysis.

useCases.targetUser: Security analysts working across multiple systems
useCases.title: Cross-platform tool installation
useCases.description: Install the same reverse engineering toolchain on Ubuntu, WSL2, macOS, or Arch Linux systems.

useCases.title: Troubleshoot missing tools
useCases.description: Diagnose why analysis commands fail and install the missing reverse engineering components.

promptTemplates.title: Basic installation
promptTemplates.prompt: Install radare2, Ghidra, GDB, QEMU, and cross-compilation toolchains on my system.
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need all core reverse engineering tools installed

promptTemplates.title: Platform-specific setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Set up reverse engineering tools on [Ubuntu/macOS/WSL2/Arch Linux].
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need platform-specific installation instructions

promptTemplates.title: Verify toolchain
promptTemplates.prompt: Verify that radare2, QEMU, and GDB are working correctly and show their versions.
promptTemplates.scenario: When you want to confirm tools are properly installed

promptTemplates.title: Cross-architecture setup
promptTemplates.prompt: Configure QEMU and sysroots for analyzing ARM and ARM64 binaries on my x86-64 system.
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need to analyze binaries from different architectures

bestPractices: Use WSL2 on Windows for best compatibility with Linux reverse engineering tools
Install sysroots before analyzing cross-architecture binaries to avoid runtime errors
Verify tool installations with version checks after setup
Configure ~/.radare2rc with appropriate analysis timeouts for large binaries
antiPatterns: Installing tools without verifying they work - always run version checks
Mounting analysis files from /tmp in Docker containers (use ~/path instead)
Skipping r2ghidra plugin installation when decompilation is needed
Running analysis without proper sysroots for the target architecture

faq.question: What tools are required for basic binary reverse engineering?
faq.answer: radare2 for disassembly and analysis, GDB with multiarch for debugging, QEMU for cross-architecture emulation, and optionally Ghidra for decompilation.

faq.question: How do I analyze ARM binaries on an x86-64 system?
faq.answer: Install qemu-user, qemu-user-static, and the ARM sysroots. Then use qemu-arm or qemu-aarch64 to execute binaries, or qemu-arm -g 1234 for remote debugging with GDB.

faq.question: Why does Ghidra decompilation fail in radare2?
faq.answer: The r2ghidra plugin is not installed. Run r2pm -ci r2ghidra to install it. Verify with r2 -qc 'pdg?' - which should return usage information.

faq.question: Can I use these tools on macOS?
faq.answer: radare2 and Ghidra work natively. QEMU lacks full user-space emulation support on macOS; use Docker with Colima for cross-arch analysis. GDB requires code signing on macOS.

faq.question: How do I verify my toolchain is working?
faq.answer: Run version checks for each tool: r2 -v, qemu-arm --version, gdb --version. Test cross-arch by running an ARM binary with qemu-arm.

faq.question: What are sysroots and why do I need them?
faq.answer: Sysroots are root filesystem libraries for target architectures. They provide the correct shared libraries that cross-compiled binaries need at runtime. Install libc6-*-cross packages for ARM.
